emergent_themes.description: Acquiring multiple delivery subscriptions through bundled services (Amazon Prime, bank benefits, mobile network perks) rather than direct payment, leading to multi-platform usage without direct cost consideration
Living location (distance from restaurants) as a primary driver for choosing delivery over dining out, making delivery a practical necessity rather than convenience choice
Household cooking responsibilities clearly divided with partner as primary cook, affecting individual relationship with food preparation and delivery ordering decisions

coding_summary: James represents a unique post-graduation case study showing how professional income transition affects delivery app usage. Key findings include his systematic acquisition of all three major delivery subscriptions through bundled services rather than direct payment, his pragmatic use of delivery as a convenience tool driven by geographic location and time constraints, and his partner-influenced household food dynamics. His ordering behavior has increased post-graduation due to higher disposable income and subscription availability, with deal-seeking behavior driving platform choice rather than brand loyalty. The interview reveals strong evidence for RO4 (post-graduation evolution) and RO2 (delivery triggers), with notable insights into subscription acquisition strategies and multi-platform arbitrage behavior.
coverage_gaps: RO1_eating_moments.how_does_accommodation_type_shape_eating - participant lives with partner in private accommodation, limited data on accommodation impact
RO3_emotional_social.is_there_stigma_around_solo_ordering_or_ - no evidence of stigma discussed in interview
